title: Germany invades Poland and the outbreak of the Second World War in Europe
summary: Before dawn on 1 September 1939 German aircraft bombed the Polish town of Wielun and the battleship Schleswig-Holstein opened fire on a Polish garrison at Westerplatte in Danzig harbour. Some 1.5 million German troops then crossed the Polish frontier in a coordinated ground and air assault. Two days later, on 3 September, Britain and France declared war on Germany. Within four weeks Poland had been overrun and partitioned with the Soviet Union under the terms of the Nazi-Soviet Pact. The Second World War in Europe had begun.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The invasion of Poland was the endpoint of six years of Adolf Hitler's foreign policy since he became German chancellor in January 1933. Rearmament had begun almost immediately, in defiance of the Versailles limits; universal military service was reintroduced in March 1935; the Rhineland was remilitarised in March 1936; and in March 1938 the German army entered Austria and Berlin declared the Anschluss with the country of Hitler's birth. Six months later, at Munich, Britain, France, and Italy handed the German-speaking Sudetenland of Czechoslovakia to Germany in exchange for a promise of peace; in March 1939 Hitler tore up the promise and occupied Prague, ending the independent Czechoslovak state.

The fall of Czechoslovakia was a turning point in the western democracies. Britain and France on 31 March 1939 gave a public guarantee to Poland, whose Free City of Danzig and 'Polish Corridor' separating East Prussia from the rest of Germany were Hitler's next targets. Through the spring and summer he pressed Poland for the return of Danzig and for extraterritorial road and rail links across the Corridor. The Polish government of Colonel Jozef Beck, encouraged by London and Paris, refused. In late April Hitler renounced the 1934 German-Polish non-aggression pact and the Anglo-German naval agreement.

The decisive diplomatic move came in the east. Britain and France conducted desultory military talks with the Soviet Union through the summer of 1939 while Joseph Stalin, distrustful of Western intentions and offered a share of eastern Europe, entertained a parallel German approach. On 23 August 1939 the German foreign minister Joachim von Ribbentrop flew to Moscow and, with Soviet foreign minister Vyacheslav Molotov, signed a non-aggression pact. A secret protocol divided eastern Europe into German and Soviet spheres of influence, allotting Finland, Estonia, Latvia, eastern Poland, and Bessarabia to the Soviet Union and western Poland and Lithuania (later revised) to Germany. The pact stunned the world and freed Hitler for a war in the west without fear of a two-front conflict. On 25 August Britain and Poland signed a formal military alliance. A last-minute Mussolini-brokered offer to reconvene at another Munich collapsed in the small hours of 1 September when a squad of SS men in Polish uniforms staged an attack on the German radio station at Gleiwitz, providing the pretext Hitler had ordered.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
At 04:45 local time on 1 September 1939 the old German training battleship Schleswig-Holstein, moored on a courtesy visit at Danzig, opened fire at point-blank range on the small Polish garrison at the Westerplatte peninsula, whose defenders under Major Henryk Sucharski would hold out for a week. Almost simultaneously German bombers of Luftflotten 1 and 4 hit airfields, railheads, and towns across western Poland; a raid on the small town of Wielun killed several hundred civilians and is often reckoned the war's first air atrocity. Fifty-five divisions of the German army, five of them armoured (Panzer) and four motorised, some 1.5 million men, crossed the Polish frontier from East Prussia, Pomerania, Silesia, and, from 3 September, Slovakia.

Britain and France issued ultimatums on 3 September demanding an immediate German withdrawal. When these expired, Neville Chamberlain announced by radio at 11:15 that morning that Britain was at war with Germany; France followed at 17:00. Australia and New Zealand also declared war on 3 September; South Africa on 6 September; Canada on 10 September. Yet no Allied ground offensive followed. French forces made only a small probe into the Saar and withdrew; the so-called Sitzkrieg or Phoney War in the west would last through the winter.

Inside Poland the campaign was short and shattering. German armour and dive-bombers, coordinated by radio in the tactic that Western journalists soon labelled Blitzkrieg, tore through Polish defensive lines and encircled entire armies west of the Vistula. Warsaw was bombed from the air from 1 September; a besieging German army opened a general assault on 25 September and the capital surrendered on 27 September. On 17 September, after a week of hesitation, the Red Army crossed the eastern Polish border in overwhelming force, meeting little resistance and taking around a quarter of a million prisoners. Polish forces, caught between two invasions and out of ammunition, continued organised fighting at Kock until 6 October. On 28 September a revised German-Soviet treaty of friendship and boundary formally partitioned Poland along the Bug River. Some 200,000 Polish soldiers were dead or wounded; roughly 700,000 were German prisoners and 250,000 Soviet. The Polish government and elements of the armed forces escaped through Romania and Hungary to France, and later Britain, where they would fight on for the rest of the war.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The occupations that followed the September campaign set the pattern of the war. In the German zone western Poland was annexed outright to the Reich, and central and southern Poland became the General Government under Nazi civilian administration. Almost immediately SS Einsatzgruppen began to murder Polish elites, priests, teachers, aristocrats, officers, and Jews, whom Nazi decrees rapidly deprived of citizenship and property; ghettos were created at Lodz in early 1940 and Warsaw in the autumn. From 1941 the industrial killing sites at Chelmno, Belzec, Sobibor, Treblinka, Majdanek, and Auschwitz-Birkenau were built or expanded on Polish soil. In the Soviet zone the NKVD deported hundreds of thousands of Polish citizens to Siberia and Kazakhstan; some 22,000 Polish officers, policemen, and intellectuals were murdered in the Katyn forest and other sites in April-May 1940.

Strategically the war widened month by month. In November 1939 the Soviet Union attacked Finland; in April 1940 Germany invaded Denmark and Norway; in May the German offensive in the west knocked France out of the war in six weeks and forced the British army off the Continent at Dunkirk. Italy joined Germany in June 1940. The Battle of Britain in the summer and autumn was the first major German defeat. In April 1941 Germany overran Yugoslavia and Greece; on 22 June 1941 it invaded the Soviet Union in Operation Barbarossa. Japan, formally allied with Germany and Italy since September 1940, struck the United States at Pearl Harbor on 7 December 1941, turning the European war into a truly global conflict.

The European war ended on 8 May 1945 with German unconditional surrender in a schoolhouse at Reims and a ceremony in Berlin. Estimates of European civilian and military deaths run between 40 and 45 million; some six million European Jews were murdered in the Holocaust. Poland, whose invasion had opened the war, lost roughly a fifth of its prewar population and finished the war under a Moscow-installed communist government.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The German invasion of Poland is the date used by almost every history of the Second World War to open its narrative, and 1 September is a national day of remembrance in Poland. Its immediate lesson, that appeasement of a totalitarian expansionist power had failed disastrously, became a defining maxim of postwar Western foreign policy, invoked at Suez in 1956, in Kosovo in 1999, and in countless debates since. Chamberlain's reputation collapsed and Winston Churchill's rose. The Nazi-Soviet Pact, which had made the invasion possible, was largely written out of Soviet history but preserved a bitter memory in the Baltic states, in Poland, and in Ukraine; its acknowledgement by Moscow in 1989 was one of the first cracks in the Soviet ideological edifice.

Militarily the campaign showed the world the potential of combined-arms mechanised warfare and radio-controlled tactical air support. German staff practice, doctrine, and equipment were studied minutely by all subsequent belligerents; the terms 'Blitzkrieg' and 'Panzer' entered English usage. The scale and speed of civilian bombing at Wielun, Warsaw, and beyond foreshadowed a war in which the distinction between front and home front would collapse.

Politically the war set in motion the second reordering of the twentieth-century world. The collapse of European empires accelerated; the United States and the Soviet Union emerged as global superpowers; the United Nations, human-rights declarations, and the Nuremberg trials were built to prevent the repetition of what the war had loosed; and the horror of the Holocaust, whose foundations were laid in occupied Poland, drove the moral shape of postwar politics from the founding of the state of Israel to the doctrine of humanitarian intervention half a century later. Every European border and almost every European institution now bears the marks of the war that began at Westerplatte.

worldContext.text: China had been fighting Japan since the Marco Polo Bridge Incident of July 1937 in what became a full-scale, brutal war. By September 1939 Japan controlled the coast from Manchuria to Guangdong; the Nationalist government under Chiang Kai-shek had retreated to Chongqing on the upper Yangtze. In August 1939 the Red Army under Georgy Zhukov decisively defeated Japanese forces at Nomonhan (Khalkhin Gol) on the Mongolian frontier, a battle that helped push Japan's strategic gaze away from Siberia and towards the resources of Southeast Asia.

The British Viceroy of India, Lord Linlithgow, declared India at war with Germany on 3 September 1939 without consulting Indian political leaders. The Indian National Congress, whose ministries controlled eight of the eleven provinces, resigned in protest and demanded a clear commitment to postwar independence. Muhammad Ali Jinnah's Muslim League seized the opening to lay the groundwork for its March 1940 Lahore Resolution and the demand for Pakistan. Some 2.5 million Indians would nonetheless serve in the British Indian Army by war's end.

The Middle East in September 1939 was a strategic prize the belligerents were already contesting. British forces held Egypt, Palestine, Transjordan, and the Suez Canal; French forces held Syria and Lebanon. The 1936 Palestinian Arab revolt had just been crushed; the White Paper of May 1939 sharply limited Jewish immigration to Palestine. In Iraq nationalist officers around Rashid Ali were eyeing an Axis opening. The Anglo-Iranian Oil Company's refinery at Abadan was already regarded as one of the war's key economic assets.

Almost all of Africa was drawn into the war by declarations from Paris, London, Lisbon, and Brussels. Some three-quarters of a million African soldiers would ultimately serve. Italian East Africa (Ethiopia, Eritrea, Somalia) under the Duke of Aosta would be swept away by Commonwealth forces in 1941. In South Africa a bitter parliamentary crisis in early September saw Jan Smuts defeat Prime Minister J. B. M. Hertzog on the question of joining Britain's war; Smuts became prime minister and led South Africa into the conflict.

The United States under Franklin D. Roosevelt was formally neutral but sympathetically inclined to the democracies. On 5 September 1939 Roosevelt proclaimed neutrality but declared that he could not ask Americans to be neutral in thought; a revision of the Neutrality Acts in November enabled 'cash and carry' arms sales to belligerents. Canada declared war on 10 September. Across Latin America, most republics remained neutral and used the Panama Declaration of October 1939 to demand a hemispheric safety zone free of belligerent activity.

Prime Minister Robert Menzies of Australia announced by radio on 3 September 1939 that, since Britain had declared war on Germany, Australia was 'also at war'; New Zealand's Prime Minister Michael Joseph Savage made a comparable declaration. Both dominions immediately began raising expeditionary forces; the 2nd AIF and 2nd NZEF would be sent to Egypt and Palestine over the winter and would fight in North Africa, Greece, and Crete before their entanglement with the Pacific War after 1941. Pacific dependencies became early strategic assets and later Japanese targets.

figures.name: Adolf Hitler
figures.role: Chancellor and Fuhrer of Nazi Germany, ordered the invasion

figures.name: Joseph Stalin
figures.role: General Secretary of the Soviet Communist Party, signed the pact and ordered the Red Army into eastern Poland on 17 September 1939

figures.name: Neville Chamberlain
figures.role: British prime minister, announced Britain's declaration of war by radio on 3 September 1939

figures.name: Edouard Daladier
figures.role: French premier who led France into the war alongside Britain

figures.name: Jozef Beck
figures.role: Polish foreign minister who resisted German demands over Danzig and the Polish Corridor

figures.name: Vyacheslav Molotov
figures.role: Soviet foreign minister and co-signatory of the 23 August 1939 non-aggression pact with Nazi Germany

images.caption: German soldiers tearing down a Polish border barrier on the morning of 1 September 1939, a widely reproduced propaganda image.
The battleship Schleswig-Holstein firing on Polish positions on the Baltic coast in September 1939.
images.credit: German propaganda company photographer, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Joachim von Ribbentrop and Joseph Stalin at the Kremlin on 23 August 1939, at the signing of the Nazi-Soviet non-aggression pact.

sources.title: Outbreak of the Second World War
